entities.name: śabdasaṃskāra
entities.cite: sabda-samskara, the perfecting or refinement of the word (the grammarian's daily work: samskara is what grammar *does* to speech)

entities.name: siddhiḥ
entities.cite: simply is siddhih paramatmanah, the attainment of the supreme self

entities.name: pravṛttitattva
entities.cite: the principle of the word's functioning, its descent from undivided source through mind and breath to audible difference and its return

entities.name: pravṛttitattvajna
entities.cite: one who knows the principle of the word's functioning

axioms.cite: sabda-samskara, the perfecting or refinement of the word (the grammarian's daily work: samskara is what grammar *does* to speech), simply is siddhih paramatmanah, the attainment of the supreme self

axioms.name: is-attainment-of
axioms.cite: simply is siddhih paramatmanah, the attainment of the supreme self

axioms.name: functioning-principle
axioms.cite: the principle of the word's functioning, its descent from undivided source through mind and breath to audible difference and its return

axioms.name: asnute
axioms.cite: tad brahma-amrtam asnute, enjoys that deathless Brahman

denials.name: asnute
denials.cite: one who knows the principle of the word's functioning, its descent from undivided source through mind and breath to audible difference and its return — tad brahma-amrtam asnute, enjoys that deathless Brahman

claims.cite: that perfecting of the word is the attainment

claims.name: is-attainment-of
claims.cite: the attainment of the supreme self

rejected_readings.label: mechanical_grammar
rejected_readings.rendering: Those who master the technical rules of grammar automatically attain the supreme self.
rejected_readings.why: Reduces sabda-samskara to mechanical linguistic skill rather than the deeper principle (pravrtti-tattva) of the word's functioning. The verse requires active knowledge (tattva-jna) of the principle, not mere grammatical competence. Also treats attainment as automatic rather than as realization.

rejected_readings.label: skip_the_principle
rejected_readings.rendering: Grammar practitioners enjoy the deathless Brahman through their practice alone.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly conditions enjoyment on knowing the principle (pravrtti-tattva-jna), not on practice or technical skill. The commentary stresses this is knowledge of 'the principle of the word's functioning, its descent and return,' which is a cognitive and metaphysical realization, not mechanical application. This reading drops the knowledge requirement (tattva-jna) and falsely attributes the enjoyment (asnute) to the practice itself rather than to the knower.

rejected_readings.label: paramatman_vs_brahma
rejected_readings.rendering: The perfecting of language is the attainment of the supreme self, separate from the deathless Brahman which is enjoyed as a different goal.
rejected_readings.why: Treats paramatman (supreme self) and brahma-amrta (deathless Brahman) as distinct end-goals rather than the same realization. The commentary frames them as continuous: attainment of the supreme self via word-perfecting and the enjoyment of brahma-amrta are manifestations of a single realization, tasted differently (recalling 'paramo rasa' of 1.12). The principle of functioning bridges them as a unified path.

notes: The verse concludes the Brahmakāṇḍa's constructive arc by collapsing means and end: sabda-samskara (the grammarian's daily work) IS siddhih (attainment), which is OF paramatman (the absolute). The knowledge of pravrtti-tattva—how the word descends from unity through mind and breath to audible form and returns—is the cognitive gate to enjoying brahma-amrta. The contract encodes this as a chain: perfecting = attainment (identity), attainment = realization-of-the-supreme (relation), knowing-the-principle = tasting-the-absolute (relation). No significant strain; the six sorts accommodate the metaphysics clearly.
